input_text:
Launched from the Cape Canaveral location, Voyager 1 commenced its mission on September 5, 1977. Voyager 2 followed sixteen days after, as NASA had assigned separate goals to the two probes. The endeavor, launched aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a core objective spanning five years: to observe the outer planets, specifically Jupiter and Saturn, during a rare astronomical alignment of these immense bodies, which occurs approximately every 176 years.

The probe arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this passage, it transmitted the initial high-resolution images of the planet's subtle ring system and documented the vigorous volcanism on its moon Io, marking the first recorded evidence of such activity originating outside of Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 made its closest pass to Saturn, successfully recording clear visuals of its rings and obtaining detailed perspectives of Titan. This flyby of Titan caused Voyager 1 to depart the solar system's plane, thereby concluding its planetary tour while simultaneously beginning its journey into interstellar space.

Guided by a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 reoriented its instruments toward Earth on February 14, 1990, successfully capturing an image of our planet from a distance approaching 6 billion kilometers. This photograph, appearing as a solitary pixel against the Sun’s diffuse brightness, became enduringly known as the Pale Blue Dot. This was the last photograph taken by the spacecraft; thereafter, the cameras were shut down to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to achieve this. It carries the Golden Record, a gold-plated, 12-inch copper phonograph disc. This disc, developed with input including Sagan's, contains greetings in 55 languages, selected musical pieces, and visuals intended for any civilization that might eventually find it. The spacecraft continues to be powered by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators using plutonium-238, whose power supply has been gradually waning due to its 87.7-year half-life.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated about 25 billion kilometers from Earth, maintaining a velocity near 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, in November 2026, it will reach the significant milestone of having traveled one light-day.
output_text:
Launched from the site at Cape Canaveral, Voyager 1 began its mission on September 5, 1977. Voyager 2 followed sixteen days later, as NASA had allocated distinct objectives to the two probes. The undertaking, which was launched aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, had a central goal that spanned five years: to observe the outer planets, namely Jupiter and Saturn, capitalizing on a rare astronomical alignment of these massive bodies, an occurrence that happens roughly every 176 years.

The probe reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this encounter, it transmitted the initial high-resolution imagery of the planet’s faint ring structure and documented the intense volcanism on its moon Io, which represented the first recorded instances of such activity originating beyond Earth. Two years following this, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 executed its closest approach to Saturn, successfully recording clear views of its rings and gathering detailed views of Titan. This close flyby of Titan caused Voyager 1 to depart the ecliptic plane of the solar system, thereby concluding its planetary tour while simultaneously beginning its journey into interstellar space.

At the suggestion of astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 redirected its instruments toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet from a distance approaching 6 billion kilometers. This photograph, appearing as a single pixel against the diffused brightness of the Sun, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This marked the final photograph taken by the spacecraft; after this, the cameras were powered down to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc. This disc, developed with input including Sagan's, contains greetings in 55 languages, selected musical compositions, and visuals intended for any civilization that might eventually discover it. The spacecraft remains powered by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators utilizing plutonium-238, whose power supply diminishes due to its 87.7-year half-life.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is located approximately 25 billion kilometers from Earth, sustaining a velocity near 61,000 kilometers per hour. Moreover, in November 2026, it will reach the notable benchmark of having traveled one light-day.
